preFix.title: Polymer clay faux coral drop earrings
preFix.subtitle: Textured polymer clay earrings shaped to mimic branching coral
preFix.excerpt: Faux coral earrings made from polymer clay with a branch-shaped form and a sandy surface texture added with fine salt. The salt dissolves during baking to leave a pitted, coral-like surface. Each earring takes about 20 minutes to shape. No kiln or wheel required.
preFix.sourceNotes: Polymer clay salt texture technique derived from first principles. Technique well-established in polymer clay community.

content.text: These earrings use fine salt scattered onto polymer clay to create the pitted surface of coral branches. The salt sits on the clay during baking and dissolves when rinsed away, leaving behind the texture. The technique works with any colour of polymer clay; terracotta orange, coral pink, and bleached white are the most convincing.

items.name: Polymer clay, terracotta or coral pink
Fine table salt or sea salt flakes

content.text: Condition the polymer clay until it is soft and pliable.

complete when the clay stretches without cracking. Roll a sausage of clay about 60 mm long and 6 mm thick. Pinch the lower two-thirds into two or three tapered branches by pulling small sections outward and narrowing them. The branches should be uneven and organic rather than perfectly symmetrical. Press a head pin into the top of the piece while it is still soft.
Sprinkle fine salt over the clay form and press it lightly into the surface with a fingertip.
slightly to embed the grains rather than just resting them on top. The salt should cover most of the surface. Place on the wooden board for baking.

Bake at the manufacturer's temperature (usually 130 degrees C) for 30 minutes. Allow to cool completely. Rinse under cool water: the salt grains wash away, leaving small pits across the surface. The pitted texture resembles the surface of dried coral. Allow to dry fully, then attach each piece to an earring hook via the head pin loop.
Use bleached white clay and paint with a diluted terracotta wash after baking for a sun-bleached effect.
Add a second smaller branch piece on a jump ring below the main piece for a stacked earring.
Use coarser sea salt for deeper pitting, or fine sand for a smoother speckled surface.

rows.fix: Rinse with warm water and use a toothbrush to remove stubborn grains. Fine table salt releases more reliably than coarse salt.
rows.cause: Clay was overbaked or salt was pressed too deeply
rows.problem: Salt stays embedded and does not wash away

rows.fix: Keep branch thickness above 3 mm. Very thin branches are too fragile for earring wear.
rows.cause: Branches were pulled too thin before baking
rows.problem: Branches break at the join point

rows.fix: Re-insert a pin and seal around it with a small collar of fresh clay, then rebake.
rows.cause: Pin was pushed in at the wrong angle or the clay shrank away from it
rows.problem: Head pin came loose after baking

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
